You said:

i want to Implement simple data engineering pipe line that contains mongodb as final distanation what is your recommendations

ChatGPT said:

For a simple but strong data engineering pipeline with MongoDB as the final destination, I recommend building something that demonstrates the core data engineering concepts clearly:

  • Data ingestion

  • Transformation

  • Validation

  • Storage

  • Scheduling/automation

  • Monitoring

Since you are aiming toward data engineering roles, the best project is one that is:

  • practical

  • easy to explain in interviews

  • uses industry tools

  • not overly complex

Recommended Simple Pipeline Architecture

Option 1 (Best for Learning + Portfolio)

API → Kafka → PySpark → MongoDB

This is one of the best beginner/intermediate pipelines because it introduces streaming concepts.

Data Source (API / CSV / IoT Simulator)
            ↓
        Kafka Producer
            ↓
          Kafka
            ↓
      PySpark Consumer
            ↓
   Data Cleaning/Transform
            ↓
         MongoDB

Why I Recommend This

It helps you practice:

SkillTool
Streaming ingestionApache Kafka
Data processingApache Spark / PySpark
NoSQL storageMongoDB
ContainersDocker
ETL conceptsPython
Real-time architectureKafka + Spark

This fits very well with your background because you already worked with Kafka, Docker, Spark, MongoDB, and AWS labs.

Suggested Project Ideas

1. IoT Sensor Pipeline (Highly Recommended)

You already like IoT projects.

Flow

Python Sensor Simulator
    ↓
Kafka Topic
    ↓
PySpark Streaming
    ↓
MongoDB

Example Data

JSON
{
  "sensor_id": "S101",
  "temperature": 34,
  "humidity": 50,
  "machine_status": "running",
  "timestamp": "2026-05-29 12:30:00"
}

Transformations You Can Apply

Inside PySpark:

  • Remove nulls

  • Convert timestamp

  • Filter invalid temperatures

  • Add alert column

Example:

Python
if temperature > 40:
    alert = "HIGH_TEMP"

MongoDB Collections

Example collection:

factory_monitoring.sensor_data

Document example:

JSON
{
  "_id": ObjectId(),
  "sensor_id": "S101",
  "temperature": 34,
  "humidity": 50,
  "machine_status": "running",
  "alert": "NORMAL",
  "processed_time": "2026-05-29 12:31:00"
}

Recommended Tech Stack

Minimum Stack

LayerTechnology
ProducerPython
BrokerApache Kafka
ProcessingPySpark
StorageMongoDB
ContainerizationDocker Compose

Recommended Docker Services

Your docker-compose can contain:

YAML
services:
  zookeeper:
  kafka:
  spark-master:
  spark-worker:
  mongodb:
  mongo-express:

Another Easier Option (If You Want Simpler)

CSV/API → Python ETL → MongoDB

Architecture:

CSV/API
   ↓
Python Pandas
   ↓
Cleaning
   ↓
MongoDB

This is easier but less impressive than Kafka + Spark.

Best APIs/Datasets

APIs

  • Weather API

  • Crypto prices

  • Fake store API

  • COVID data

  • OpenAQ air quality API

Dataset Ideas

  • Taxi trips

  • E-commerce orders

  • IoT sensors

  • Website logs

  • Smart factory data

What Makes the Project Strong on Your CV

Add these concepts:

1. Real-Time Streaming

Using Kafka + Spark Streaming.

2. Data Validation

Example:

Python
if humidity < 0:
    discard row

3. Fault Tolerance

Kafka offsets + checkpointing.

4. Dockerized Environment

Very important for interviews.

5. Logging

Use Python logging:

Python
import logging

6. Monitoring Dashboard (Optional Bonus)

You can add:

  • Grafana

  • Mongo Express

Recommended Folder Structure

project/
│
├── producer/
│   └── producer.py
│
├── spark/
│   └── streaming_job.py
│
├── mongodb/
│
├── docker-compose.yml
│
├── requirements.txt
│
└── README.md

What I Would Personally Recommend For You

Based on your background:

Build This

“Real-Time Factory Monitoring Pipeline”

Using:

  • Apache Kafka

  • Apache Spark

  • MongoDB

  • Docker

Features

  • Simulated machine sensors

  • Real-time ingestion

  • Temperature anomaly detection

  • Store processed alerts in MongoDB

  • Dashboard using Mongo Express or Grafana

This is:

  • interview-friendly

  • realistic

  • not too advanced

  • aligned with data engineering roles

Extra Recommendation

If you want your project to look more professional:

Add Medallion Architecture

Even simple version:

Raw Data  →  Clean Data  →  Aggregated Data
(Bronze)     (Silver)        (Gold)

You can implement this using different MongoDB collections.

Example:

raw_sensor_data
clean_sensor_data
daily_machine_summary

Future Improvements

Later you can extend it with:

  • AWS S3

  • Airflow scheduling

  • Elasticsearch

  • Grafana dashboards

  • Machine learning anomaly detection

  • CI/CD

  • Kubernetes

Final Recommendation

For the best balance between:

  • simplicity

  • learning

  • portfolio value

  • interview impact

Build:

Python Producer
    → Kafka
    → PySpark Streaming
    → MongoDB

with an IoT/factory monitoring use case.


You said:

what about the data ?

ChatGPT said:

For the data, you have 3 good options depending on what you want to demonstrate in the pipeline.

Since your final destination is MongoDB, the best data is usually:

  • semi-structured

  • JSON-like

  • changing over time

  • streaming-friendly

That is exactly where MongoDB shines.

Best Recommendation for You

Use Simulated IoT / Factory Sensor Data

This fits perfectly with:

  • Kafka streaming

  • PySpark transformations

  • MongoDB document storage

  • your IoT pipeline idea

Example Data Structure

Raw Sensor Event

JSON
{
  "sensor_id": "M101",
  "machine_type": "CNC",
  "temperature": 37.5,
  "humidity": 42,
  "vibration": 0.32,
  "power_consumption": 1500,
  "worker_id": "W12",
  "safety_helmet": true,
  "status": "running",
  "timestamp": "2026-05-29T14:30:00"
}

Why This Data Is Excellent

You can demonstrate:

ConceptExample
StreamingKafka events
ValidationRemove negative temperature
TransformationConvert timestamps
Business rulesDetect overheating
AggregationAvg temp per machine
AlertsUnsafe worker detection
Schema evolutionAdd new sensor columns later
MongoDB flexibilityDifferent machines can send different fields

Recommended Pipeline Logic

Step 1 — Generate Data

Create a Python producer that generates fake sensor events every few seconds.

Example:

Python
while True:
    data = {
        "sensor_id": random.choice(["M101", "M102"]),
        "temperature": random.randint(20, 50),
        "humidity": random.randint(30, 80)
    }

Send to Kafka topic:

factory-sensors

Step 2 — Kafka Stores Stream

Kafka acts as the message broker.

Each message is JSON.

Step 3 — PySpark Streaming

Read from Kafka:

Python
df = spark.readStream.format("kafka")

Step 4 — Apply Transformations

Example Rules

Detect high temperature

Python
when(col("temperature") > 40, "HIGH_TEMP")

Detect unsafe worker

Python
when(col("safety_helmet") == False, "SAFETY_ALERT")

Clean invalid rows

Python
filter(col("temperature") > 0)

Step 5 — Store in MongoDB

Save processed data into MongoDB collection:

smart_factory.processed_sensor_data

Example Final MongoDB Document

JSON
{
  "sensor_id": "M101",
  "temperature": 45,
  "humidity": 60,
  "status": "running",
  "alert": "HIGH_TEMP",
  "processed_time": "2026-05-29T14:35:00"
}

How to Generate the Data

Option 1 — Python Faker (Recommended)

Use:

Python
from faker import Faker

and random.

This is the best option.

Option 2 — Real Public Dataset

If you want real data instead of simulated.

Good datasets:

IoT Dataset

  • machine sensors

  • predictive maintenance

  • smart manufacturing

You can find datasets on:

  • Kaggle

  • UCI Machine Learning Repository

Recommended Real Dataset Types

DatasetWhy Good
Predictive maintenanceRealistic industrial use case
Smart factory sensorsMatches streaming pipeline
Taxi tripsLarge-scale processing
Website clickstreamEvent streaming
E-commerce ordersBusiness analytics

Option 3 — API Streaming

Instead of fake data.

Example APIs:

  • weather API

  • crypto prices

  • air quality sensors

Pipeline:

API → Kafka → Spark → MongoDB

This is also very good.

My Recommendation For You

Start with:

Fake IoT Streaming Data

Because:

  • easiest to control

  • easiest to explain

  • easiest to expand

  • best for Kafka + MongoDB

  • demonstrates engineering concepts clearly

Add Realism to the Data

You can make the pipeline look professional by adding:

Machine Problems

{

  "vibration": 0.95,
  "machine_status": "critical"
}

Worker Safety

JSON

  "helmet_detected": false
}

}

Best Collections in MongoDB

You can create multiple collections:

CollectionPurpose
raw_sensor_dataunprocessed stream
cleaned_sensor_datavalidated records
alertsanomalies
machine_summaryaggregated analytics

This looks very professional in interviews.

Simple Architecture

Python Producer
      ↓
Kafka Topic
      ↓
PySpark Streaming
      ↓
MongoDB Collections

Bonus Recommendation

If you want your project to stand out:

Add Schema Evolution

Example:
Day 1:

JSON
{
  "temperature": 35
}

Day 5:

JSON
{
  "temperature": 35,
  "pressure": 110
}

MongoDB handles this naturally.

This demonstrates:

  • NoSQL flexibility

  • real-world evolving pipelines

Very strong concept for interviews.

ok now lets start with this option Recommended Simple Pipeline Architecture Option 1 (Best for Learning + Portfolio) API → Kafka → PySpark → MongoDB and i will use pyhtin faker lets start step by step and do not jump to another step till i ask you

ChatGPT said:

Perfect. We'll build it one step at a time.

Project Architecture

Faker Data Generator
        ↓
Kafka Producer
        ↓
Kafka Topic
        ↓
PySpark Streaming Consumer
        ↓
MongoDB

Since you asked not to jump ahead, we'll focus only on Step 1: Generate Fake Data using Faker.

Step 1: Create the Data Generator

Goal

Generate realistic machine sensor events that will later be sent to Kafka.

Install Required Packages

Bash
pip install faker

Verify installation:

Bash
pip show faker

Create data_generator.py

Python
from faker import Faker
import random
from datetime import datetime

fake = Faker()

def generate_sensor_data():
    return {
        "sensor_id": f"M{random.randint(100,999)}",
        "machine_type": random.choice([
            "CNC",
            "Lathe",
            "Drill",
            "Conveyor"
        ]),
        "temperature": round(random.uniform(20, 50), 2),
        "humidity": round(random.uniform(30, 80), 2),
        "vibration": round(random.uniform(0.1, 1.0), 2),
        "power_consumption": random.randint(1000, 5000),
        "worker_id": f"W{random.randint(1,50)}",
        "safety_helmet": random.choice([True, False]),
        "status": random.choice([
            "running",
            "idle",
            "maintenance"
        ]),
        "timestamp": datetime.now().isoformat()
    }

print(generate_sensor_data())

Run the Script

Bash
python data_generator.py

Example output:

Python
{
    'sensor_id': 'M451',
    'machine_type': 'CNC',
    'temperature': 34.72,
    'humidity': 58.31,
    'vibration': 0.45,
    'power_consumption': 2150,
    'worker_id': 'W18',
    'safety_helmet': True,
    'status': 'running',
    'timestamp': '2026-06-02T18:25:10.123456'
}

Why These Fields?

FieldPurpose
sensor_idIdentify machine
machine_typeMachine category
temperatureMonitor overheating
humidityEnvironmental monitoring
vibrationDetect machine issues
power_consumptionEnergy analysis
worker_idWorker tracking
safety_helmetSafety compliance
statusMachine state
timestampEvent time

Small Improvement

Instead of generating one record, generate a stream of records:

Python
while True:
    print(generate_sensor_data())

Don't run this yet—we'll decide in the next step whether to print continuously or produce directly to Kafka.

For now:

  1. Create the file.

  2. Run it successfully.

  3. Show me the output (or any error).

Then we'll move to the next step only after you confirm.


You said:

everyb thing is working correctly now lets continue

ChatGPT said:

Great.

Step 2: Set Up Kafka

The goal of this step is:

Faker Generator
      ↓
Kafka Producer
      ↓
Kafka Topic

We are not writing any producer code yet. First, we make sure Kafka is running and create the topic.

2.1 Check if Kafka Is Running

If you're using Docker, run:

Bash
docker ps

You should see containers similar to:

zookeeper
kafka

If Kafka is not running, start your docker-compose:

Bash
docker compose up -d

2.2 Verify Kafka Is Reachable

Enter the Kafka container:

Bash
docker exec -it kafka bash

or

Bash
docker exec -it kafka sh

(depending on the image)

2.3 List Existing Topics

Inside the Kafka container:

Bash
kafka-topics \
--bootstrap-server localhost:9092 \
--list

You may see nothing if this is a fresh Kafka instance.

2.4 Create a Topic

Create a topic named:

factory-sensors

Command:

Bash
kafka-topics \
--bootstrap-server localhost:9092 \
--create \
--topic factory-sensors \
--partitions 1 \
--replication-factor 1

Expected output:

Created topic factory-sensors.

2.5 Verify Topic Creation

Run:

Bash
kafka-topics \
--bootstrap-server localhost:9092 \
--describe \
--topic factory-sensors

Expected result:

Topic: factory-sensors
PartitionCount: 1
ReplicationFactor: 1

2.6 Test with a Console Producer

Still inside the Kafka container:

Bash
kafka-console-producer \
--bootstrap-server localhost:9092 \
--topic factory-sensors

Type:

hello

Press Enter.

2.7 Open a Consumer in Another Terminal

Open another terminal and enter the Kafka container again.

Run:

Bash
kafka-console-consumer \
--bootstrap-server localhost:9092 \
--topic factory-sensors \
--from-beginning

You should see:

hello

This confirms:

Producer → Kafka Topic → Consumer

is working correctly.

Do these Kafka checks and tell me:

  1. Are you using Docker Compose for Kafka?

  2. Did the factory-sensors topic get created successfully?

  3. Were you able to send "hello" and receive it in the consumer?

Once confirmed, we'll write the Python Kafka Producer that sends your Faker sensor data to the topic.

ChatGPT said:

Your Kafka setup looks good and is actually better than a single-broker setup because you're running a 3-broker KRaft cluster.

Before we write any producer code, let's verify the cluster.

Step 2.1 Verify Containers

Run:

Bash
docker ps

You should see:

kafka-1
kafka-2
kafka-3
kafka-ui

Step 2.2 Enter Broker 1

Bash
docker exec -it kafka-1 bash

If bash is unavailable:

Bash
docker exec -it kafka-1 sh

Step 2.3 List Topics

Since you're using Kafka 4.0 in KRaft mode, run:

Bash
kafka-topics.sh \
--bootstrap-server kafka-1:9092 \
--list

If that doesn't work, check the available commands:

Bash
ls /opt/bitnami/kafka/bin

and tell me the output.

Step 2.4 Create Our Topic

Create:

factory-sensors

Command:

Bash
kafka-topics.sh \
--bootstrap-server kafka-1:9092 \
--create \
--topic factory-sensors \
--partitions 3 \
--replication-factor 3

Why 3 partitions?

Because you already configured:

YAML
KAFKA_CFG_NUM_PARTITIONS=3

and we want to utilize all brokers.

Step 2.5 Describe the Topic

Bash
kafka-topics.sh \
--bootstrap-server kafka-1:9092 \
--describe \
--topic factory-sensors

You should see something similar to:

Topic: factory-sensors
PartitionCount: 3
ReplicationFactor: 3

and partition leaders distributed across brokers.

Step 2.6 Verify in Kafka UI

Open:

http://localhost:12000

You should see:

local-kafka-cluster

Then:

Topics
    └── factory-sensors

Stop Here

Don't write the producer yet.

Run the commands above and tell me:

  1. Did kafka-topics.sh --list work?

  2. Did you create factory-sensors successfully?

  3. Can you see the topic in Kafka UI?

Once confirmed, we'll move to Step 3: Python Kafka Producer (Faker → Kafka).


You said:

why are you using port 9092

ChatGPT said:

Good question.

I'm using 9092 inside the Docker network, not from your host machine.

In your configuration, each broker has two listeners:

Internal Listener

YAML
KAFKA_CFG_LISTENERS=INTERNAL://:9092,...

and

YAML
KAFKA_CFG_ADVERTISED_LISTENERS=INTERNAL://kafka-1:9092,...

This means containers communicate with each other using:

kafka-1:9092
kafka-2:9092
kafka-3:9092

These addresses are valid inside the Docker network (kafka-net).

External Listener

For clients running on your Windows machine (VS Code, Python scripts, Spark outside Docker), you exposed:

Broker 1:

YAML
ports:
  - "9094:9094"
YAML
EXTERNAL://localhost:9094

Broker 2:

YAML
ports:
  - "9095:9094"
YAML
EXTERNAL://localhost:9095

Broker 3:

YAML
ports:
  - "9096:9094"
YAML
EXTERNAL://localhost:9096

So from your host machine, you connect using:

Python
bootstrap_servers=[
    "localhost:9094",
    "localhost:9095",
    "localhost:9096"
]

Which Port Should We Use?

If running commands inside Kafka containers

Use:

Bash
--bootstrap-server kafka-1:9092

because the container can see the internal listener.

If running Python Producer on your laptop

Use:

Python
localhost:9094
localhost:9095
localhost:9096

because the producer is outside Docker.

Our Project

Since your Faker generator will likely run from VS Code on Windows/WSL (outside Docker), later our producer configuration will be:

Python
bootstrap_servers = [
    "localhost:9094",
    "localhost:9095",
    "localhost:9096"
]

Before moving forward, tell me:

  • Are you running Python directly on Windows/WSL?

  • Or do you plan to run the producer inside a Docker container?

That determines which bootstrap server we should use throughout the project.


You said:

ok lets start with kafka producer

ChatGPT said:

Perfect. Since we're moving step by step, we'll do only this step:

Faker Data Generator
        ↓
Kafka Producer
        ↓
factory-sensors Topic

We will not touch Spark or MongoDB yet.

Step 3.1 Install Kafka Python Library

Install the Kafka client:

Bash
pip install kafka-python

Verify:

Bash
pip show kafka-python

Step 3.2 Create a Producer Script

Create:

producer.py

Step 3.3 Convert Faker Data to JSON

Update the code as follows:

Python
from faker import Faker
import random
from datetime import datetime
import json

fake = Faker()

def generate_sensor_data():
    return {
        "sensor_id": f"M{random.randint(100,999)}",
        "machine_type": random.choice([
            "CNC",
            "Lathe",
            "Drill",
            "Conveyor"
        ]),
        "temperature": round(random.uniform(20, 50), 2),
        "humidity": round(random.uniform(30, 80), 2),
        "vibration": round(random.uniform(0.1, 1.0), 2),
        "power_consumption": random.randint(1000, 5000),
        "worker_id": f"W{random.randint(1,50)}",
        "safety_helmet": random.choice([True, False]),
        "status": random.choice([
            "running",
            "idle",
            "maintenance"
        ]),
        "timestamp": datetime.now().isoformat()
    }

data = generate_sensor_data()

print(json.dumps(data, indent=2))

Run it and confirm the JSON prints correctly.

Step 3.4 Create the Kafka Producer

Add:

Python
from kafka import KafkaProducer

Create the producer:

Python
producer = KafkaProducer(
    bootstrap_servers=[
        "localhost:9094",
        "localhost:9095",
        "localhost:9096"
    ],
    value_serializer=lambda v: json.dumps(v).encode("utf-8")
)

Step 3.5 Send One Message

Add:

Python
data = generate_sensor_data()

producer.send(
    "factory-sensors",
    value=data
)

producer.flush()

print("Message sent:")
print(data)

Full test:

Python
from faker import Faker
from kafka import KafkaProducer
import random
from datetime import datetime
import json

fake = Faker()

producer = KafkaProducer(
    bootstrap_servers=[
        "localhost:9094",
        "localhost:9095",
        "localhost:9096"
    ],
    value_serializer=lambda v: json.dumps(v).encode("utf-8")
)

def generate_sensor_data():
    return {
        "sensor_id": f"M{random.randint(100,999)}",
        "temperature": round(random.uniform(20, 50), 2),
        "humidity": round(random.uniform(30, 80), 2),
        "timestamp": datetime.now().isoformat()
    }

data = generate_sensor_data()

producer.send(
    "factory-sensors",
    value=data
)

producer.flush()

print("Message sent:")
print(data)

Step 3.6 Verify the Message Reached Kafka

Open Kafka UI:

http://localhost:12000

Navigate:

Topics
   → factory-sensors

or use a console consumer from a Kafka container:

Bash
kafka-console-consumer.sh \
--bootstrap-server kafka-1:9092 \
--topic factory-sensors \
--from-beginning

You should see a JSON message similar to:

JSON
{
  "sensor_id":"M234",
  "temperature":31.5,
  "humidity":58.2,
  "timestamp":"2026-06-02T..."
}

Run the producer once and tell me:

  1. Did the script execute successfully?

  2. Did you get any Kafka connection errors?

  3. Can you see the message in Kafka UI or a consumer?

Once that works, we'll make it send continuous streaming events instead of a single message.


You said:

what if i want to seperate data_generator and producer each one in different script

ChatGPT said:

That's actually a better design.

In real projects, you usually separate:

  • Data generation logic

  • Kafka producer logic

  • Spark consumer logic

  • Database logic

A clean structure would be:

project/
│
├── data_generator.py
├── producer.py
│
├── spark/
│   └── consumer.py
│
└── requirements.txt

data_generator.py

This file is responsible only for generating data.

Python
from faker import Faker
import random
from datetime import datetime

fake = Faker()

def generate_sensor_data():
    return {
        "sensor_id": f"M{random.randint(100,999)}",
        "machine_type": random.choice([
            "CNC",
            "Lathe",
            "Drill",
            "Conveyor"
        ]),
        "temperature": round(random.uniform(20, 50), 2),
        "humidity": round(random.uniform(30, 80), 2),
        "vibration": round(random.uniform(0.1, 1.0), 2),
        "power_consumption": random.randint(1000, 5000),
        "worker_id": f"W{random.randint(1,50)}",
        "safety_helmet": random.choice([True, False]),
        "status": random.choice([
            "running",
            "idle",
            "maintenance"
        ]),
        "timestamp": datetime.now().isoformat()
    }

Notice:

  • No Kafka code.

  • No print statements.

  • Only data generation.

producer.py

This file imports the generator and sends data to Kafka.

Python
from kafka import KafkaProducer
from data_generator import generate_sensor_data
import json

producer = KafkaProducer(
    bootstrap_servers=[
        "localhost:9094",
        "localhost:9095",
        "localhost:9096"
    ],
    value_serializer=lambda v: json.dumps(v).encode("utf-8")
)

data = generate_sensor_data()

producer.send(
    "factory-sensors",
    value=data
)

producer.flush()

print("Message sent:")
print(data)

Why This Is Better

Suppose later you want:

Unit testing

Python
data = generate_sensor_data()

You can test it without Kafka.

Another destination

Instead of Kafka:

Python
data = generate_sensor_data()

collection.insert_one(data)

You can reuse the same generator.

Multiple producers

You might later have:

api_producer.py
sensor_producer.py
orders_producer.py

all using different generators.

Small Improvement

In producer.py, wrap the sending logic in a function:

Python
def send_message():
    data = generate_sensor_data()

    producer.send(
        "factory-sensors",
        value=data
    )

    producer.flush()

    print(data)

This will make it easier when we later convert it into a continuous stream.

For now:

  1. Keep generate_sensor_data() in data_generator.py.

  2. Create producer.py that imports it.

  3. Run producer.py.

  4. Verify a message arrives in the factory-sensors topic.

After that, we'll make the producer send a new sensor event every few seconds, simulating a real stream.


You said:

ok lets continue

ChatGPT said:

Excellent. Assuming your single-message producer is working, the next step is to turn it into a continuous streaming producer.

Step 4: Stream Data Continuously to Kafka

Current flow:

Faker → Producer → Kafka (1 message)

New flow:

Faker → Producer → Kafka (message every few seconds)

Update producer.py

Import time:

Python
import time

Send Messages in a Loop

Replace the single send with:

Python
while True:
    data = generate_sensor_data()

    producer.send(
        "factory-sensors",
        value=data
    )

    producer.flush()

    print("Sent:", data)

    time.sleep(5)

This will:

  1. Generate a sensor record.

  2. Send it to Kafka.

  3. Print it.

  4. Wait 5 seconds.

  5. Repeat forever.

Full Example

Python
from kafka import KafkaProducer
from data_generator import generate_sensor_data
import json
import time

producer = KafkaProducer(
    bootstrap_servers=[
        "localhost:9094",
        "localhost:9095",
        "localhost:9096"
    ],
    value_serializer=lambda v: json.dumps(v).encode("utf-8")
)

while True:
    data = generate_sensor_data()

    producer.send(
        "factory-sensors",
        value=data
    )

    producer.flush()

    print("Sent:", data)

    time.sleep(5)

Verify Messages Are Arriving

Option 1: Kafka UI

Open:

http://localhost:12000

Check the factory-sensors topic and watch messages arrive.

Option 2: Console Consumer

From a Kafka container:

Bash
kafka-console-consumer.sh \
--bootstrap-server kafka-1:9092 \
--topic factory-sensors

You should see new JSON messages appearing every 5 seconds.

Small Optimization (Optional)

Right now we're calling:

Python
producer.flush()

after every message.

For testing, that's fine.

Later, when sending many events, we can remove frequent flushes and let Kafka batch records more efficiently.

Stop Here

Before moving to Spark:

  • Run the continuous producer.

  • Confirm messages are continuously arriving in Kafka.

  • Tell me if you're checking them through Kafka UI or a console consumer.

Once that's working, the next step will be PySpark Streaming Consumer (Kafka → Spark).


You said:

ok lets continue

ChatGPT said:

Perfect. Since we're moving step by step, we'll now focus only on PySpark consuming data from Kafka.

Current pipeline:

Faker
   ↓
Kafka Producer
   ↓
Kafka Topic (factory-sensors)

Goal of this step:

Faker
   ↓
Kafka Producer
   ↓
Kafka Topic
   ↓
PySpark Streaming Consumer

We are NOT touching MongoDB yet.

Step 5.1 Add Spark to Docker Compose

Before writing code, tell me:

Are you planning to run Spark:

Option A

On your machine (Windows/WSL)

or

Option B

Inside Docker containers

I strongly recommend Option B (Docker) because:

  • Kafka is already in Docker

  • MongoDB will be in Docker later

  • Networking becomes easier

  • More realistic architecture

Step 5.2 Before Writing Any Spark Code

I need to know whether you already have Spark running.

Do you currently have any of these services?

YAML
spark-master:
spark-worker:

in your docker-compose?

Or is your compose file currently only:

YAML
kafka-1
kafka-2
kafka-3
kafka-ui

Reply with one of these:

1.

I want Spark in Docker and I don't have Spark services yet.

2.

I already have Spark containers.

3.

I want to run Spark locally on my machine.

Once you answer, we'll set up Spark correctly and then create the first streaming consumer.


You said:

no i will use spark locally

ChatGPT said:

Perfect. Since you'll run PySpark locally, we'll keep Kafka in Docker and Spark on your machine.

Current architecture:

Faker Producer (Local)
        ↓
Kafka Cluster (Docker)
        ↓
PySpark Consumer (Local)

Step 5.1 Verify Spark Installation

Open a terminal and run:

Bash
spark-submit --version

or

Bash
pyspark

You should see the Spark version.

Tell me:

  • Spark version

  • Hadoop version (if shown)

before we continue.

Step 5.2 Download Kafka Connector (Important)

PySpark cannot read Kafka directly without the Kafka connector.

The connector version must match your Spark version.

For example:

SparkKafka Package
3.5.xspark-sql-kafka-0-10_2.12:3.5.x
4.0.xspark-sql-kafka-0-10_2.13:4.0.x

So first I need your Spark version.

Step 5.3 Create the Consumer Script

We'll create:

consumer.py

but don't write it yet until we verify the Spark version.

The consumer will:

  1. Connect to Kafka

localhost:9094
localhost:9095
localhost:9096

  1. Read from topic

factory-sensors

  1. Parse JSON messages

  2. Print them to the console

Example output:

+---------+-----------+--------+
|sensor_id|temperature|humidity|
+---------+-----------+--------+
|M101     |35.6       |45.2    |
|M102     |41.2       |52.1    |
+---------+-----------+--------+

Stop Here

Run:

Bash
spark-submit --version

and send me the output.

Then we'll write the correct Kafka-to-Spark streaming consumer with the matching connector version.

Before we continue to Kafka → Spark, we need to install Spark.

Step 5.1 Check Java First

Run:

Bash
java -version

Send me the output.

Spark requires Java, and it's better to verify that before downloading anything.

Expected Output

Something like:

openjdk version "17.x.x"

or

java version "11.x.x"

If you get:

'java' is not recognized...

then we'll install Java first.

After Java Is Confirmed

We'll do:

  1. Install Spark

  2. Configure SPARK_HOME

  3. Add Spark to PATH

  4. Verify pyspark

  5. Create the Kafka consumer

Run:

Bash
java -version

and send me the result.

PS G:\IOTPipeline> java -version java version "26.0.1" 2026-04-21 Java(TM) SE Runtime Environment (build 26.0.1+8-34) Java HotSpot(TM) 64-Bit Server VM (build 26.0.1+8-34, mixed mode, sharing) PS G:\IOTPipeline>

Great. Java is installed and working.

Java 26
✓ Installed

One thing to note: Java 26 is very new. Some Spark versions may not be fully tested against it. If we encounter issues later, we may need to install Java 17, but let's proceed first.

Step 5.2 Install PySpark

Since you're running Spark locally and using Python, the easiest approach is to install PySpark via pip.

Run:

Bash
pip install pyspark

After it finishes, verify:

Bash
pip show pyspark

You should see something like:

Name: pyspark
Version: 4.x.x

Step 5.3 Verify Installation

Run:

Bash
python

Then:

Python
import pyspark
print(pyspark.__version__)

Expected:

4.0.0

(or another version number)

Then exit Python:

Python
exit()

Stop Here

Send me:

Bash
pip show pyspark

output

or

Python

print(pyspark.__version__)

output.

Once I know the exact PySpark version, we'll create the Kafka consumer with the correct Kafka connector package.
